growth_records.strain: Mpn (M129-derived laboratory strain, not explicitly re-stated in extractable text)
growth_records.medium: Hayflick rich medium (cited: Yus et al, 2009)
growth_records.cite: Weber et al. Comprehensive quantitative modeling of translation efficiency in a genome-reduced bacterium. Molecular Systems Biology (2023). [PMC10568206]
growth_records.snippet: Mpn, with a doubling time of 8 h, likely did not experience such a pressure which might explain its lower frequency of SD, which increases in faster dividing mycoplasma species.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time of 8 h for Mpn is stated as a background/contextual fact in the Discussion (used to compare against E. coli Dt=0.7h and yeast Dt=100min from other cited studies), without restating culture medium, temperature or oxygen conditions in the extractable text of this paper (methods section for Mpn cell growth/culturing was not found in the accessible XML text). growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/8 = 0.0866 h-1, calculated from the reported doubling time (formula: mu = ln2/Dt, Dt=8h). Confidence low because the value is a passing citation-style statement rather than this paper's own detailed growth measurement, and medium/temperature/strain designation are not recoverable from the extracted text. [metadata completed from paper Methods: medium]

growth_records.cite: Burgos et al. Protein quality control and regulated proteolysis in the genome-reduced organism Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Molecular Systems Biology (2020). DOI:10.15252/msb.20209530. [PMC7737663]
growth_records.snippet: To take into account the dilution rate due to cell growth, we also computed the growth rate of the cells in exponential phase by measuring protein amounts along the growth... Exponential fit... yielded an average doublin
growth_records.notes: Doubling time (8.63 ± 0.13 h) is directly reported, derived from an exponential fit of protein-biomass time points (3,6,8,12,18,24,30,36,48 h) during SILAC pulse-chase growth of wild-type M. pneumoniae M129 in Hayflick medium at 37C/5% CO2, tissue-culture flasks (batch, unagitated). growth_rate_per_h calculated by curator as mu = ln2 / doubling_time = 0.6931/8.63 = 0.0803 h-1 (formula applied to the paper's reported doubling time; mu itself not stated verbatim in text). Full Hayflick medium composition not given in this paper (cited to Yus et al. 2009), so medium_composition left empty. Oxygen status not explicitly stated beyond '5% CO2' atmosphere (M. pneumoniae cultures are typically grown in ambient air + CO2, but the paper does not state aerobic/anaerobic explicitly), so left null rather than assumed. [metadata completed from paper Methods: oxygen]

growth_records.strain: M129 (inferred; standard lab strain used in the associated experimental work of Yus et al 2009 on which the model is based; not explicitly restated in this article)
growth_records.medium: Hayflick medium supplemented with 55 mM glucose (rich medium); also a chemically defined medium per Yus et al 2009 (Supplementary Table S5)

up.method: Metabolite quantification (non-linear/sigmoidal fits to glucose consumption over a 96 h batch time course, Supplementary Figure S5B-E); used to define carbon-uptake model constraints (model units mmol x g(cells)^-1 x h^-1); numeric rate reported only in Supplementary figures/tables

sec.method: Metabolite quantification over 96 h batch time course (Supplementary Figure S5); lactic-acid production dominates during late growth; numeric rate in Supplement

Metabolite quantification over 96 h batch time course (Supplementary Figure S5E); acetic-acid production favored during early growth (4 ATP/glucose) then shifts to lactic acid; acetate-production constraint fitted to experimentally determined lactic:acetic ratio; numeric rate in Supplement

growth_records.cite: Wodke JAH et al. Dissecting the energy metabolism in Mycoplasma pneumoniae through genome-scale metabolic modeling. Molecular Systems Biology (2013). DOI:10.1038/msb.2013.6. [PMC3658275]
growth_records.snippet: Batch culture growth experiments on glucose lead to a measurable decrease in medium pH (pH 7.8-5.5 during a 4-day growth course) due to secretion of lactic and acetic acid (Yus et al, 2009). A shift from energetically favorable acetic acid
growth_records.notes: Growth: in vivo doubling time during exponential phase reported as ~20 h ('values measured for M. pneumoniae (20 h; Figure 3A)'); mu computed mu=ln2/td=0.693/20=0.0347 /h (growth_method=calculated from measured doubling time). Model in-silico doubling times were 2.3-3.8 h but these are PREDICTED (not experimental) and excluded. Substrate uptake (glucose) and product secretion (lactate, acetate) were MEASURED experimentally over a 96 h batch time course and fitted with sigmoidal functions (Supplementary Figure S5B-E) to derive model constraints in units mmol x g(cells)^-1 x h^-1; however the explicit numeric rate values reside in the Supplementary material (not in the main text provided), so rate fields are null with is_experimental=true. Medium: rich Hayflick medium + 55 mM glucose (pH experiment) and a chemically defined medium (Yus et al 2009, Supplementary Table S5); full Hayflick base composition not enumerated in this article. Temperature not explicitly stated (M. pneumoniae standard is 37C but not written, left null). pH starts ~7.8 and falls to 5.5 over the 4-day batch. No experimental 13C-MFA net-flux numbers reported (13C labelling was used qualitatively to confirm glycolytic intermediate labelling; all quantitative fluxes are FBA model predictions), so mfa_fluxes empty. Strain not explicitly named in text; iJW145 model built on Yus et al 2009 which used strain M129.
